medications.indication_rationale: Careful, gradual IV 0.9% sodium chloride is the MAINSTAY of HHS treatment — it restores intravascular volume, lowers glucose and osmolality gradually and improves consciousness before insulin is considered. This is hospital-administered; in telehealth the AI Doctor cannot give IV fluids and must escalate (000) so this is delivered by the ambulance/hospital team. No specific rate/volume is given in the source, so none is stated here.
contraindications_in_this_case: Overly rapid correction risks cerebral oedema and central pontine myelinolysis — replacement must be gradual, which is why it is hospital-titrated, not a home measure

medications.indication_rationale: In HHS, low-dose IV insulin is introduced ONLY after fluid resuscitation is under way and once glucose stops falling with fluids alone; premature or high-dose insulin causes dangerous osmotic/fluid shifts. Hospital-only; the AI Doctor does not initiate insulin. No dose is provided in the source, so none is stated.
contraindications_in_this_case: Do not give before intravenous fluids are running
Requires potassium monitoring — insulin drives potassium intracellularly

medications.drug_name: Empirical antibiotic for the precipitant (urinary source)
medications.indication_rationale: Treating the probable precipitating urinary infection is part of HHS management; agent and dose are chosen in hospital after cultures and are not specified in the source, so no dose is stated here.

Metformin must be WITHHELD during this acute illness — in severe dehydration, hypotension and likely acute kidney injury it carries a risk of lactic acidosis. It is one of the patient's home medicines and should be paused, not continued, through the acute event.
medications.deprescribing_note: Withhold during acute illness/AKI; review and only restart once renal function and hydration have recovered in hospital.
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Metformin + acute kidney injury/dehydration — lactic acidosis risk in this presentation

medications.indication_rationale: Gliclazide should also be WITHHELD acutely: with poor oral intake it risks hypoglycaemia, and glucose is being controlled by fluids then titrated insulin in hospital. It is a home medicine to pause during the emergency, not part of acute treatment.
medications.deprescribing_note: Withhold during acute illness and reduced oral intake; hospital team manages glucose with fluids and insulin.
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: Gliclazide + reduced oral intake — hypoglycaemia risk

medications.drug_name: Oral rehydration forced by mouth in a drowsy patient
medications.indication_rationale: Do NOT instruct staff to push oral fluids into a barely-rousable, obtunded patient — the aspiration risk is high and it will not correct the deficit. Rehydration must be intravenous in hospital. Small sips only if he becomes fully alert and can protect his airway.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Facility 'sick-day' plan: check blood glucose more frequently at the first sign of infection or reduced intake, and escalate early if readings climb or the resident becomes drowsy.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early detection of rising glucose during an intercurrent illness prevents the slow slide into a hyperosmolar emergency.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Ensure diabetes medicines are given reliably and that a clear sick-day rule (which tablets to hold, when to seek help) is documented in the care plan.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Missed medication plus illness was a contributor; structured administration and sick-day rules reduce recurrence.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Support regular fluid intake for a dependent resident, with staff prompting and assistance, especially during hot weather or illness.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Maintaining hydration is central to preventing hyperosmolar states in frail older diabetics.

patient_education_points.point: His blood sugar is dangerously high, which is pulling large amounts of water out of his body and making him severely dehydrated — this needs urgent hospital care right now.
The hospital will give fluids into a vein slowly over hours to days to rehydrate him and bring the sugar down carefully and safely — it is deliberately gradual to protect the brain.

In future, checking his sugar and helping him drink more the moment a minor infection starts helps stop his blood becoming dangerously thick and concentrated. (Suitable for a brief SMS summary to the facility.)

follow_up_plan.interval: immediate emergency transfer now; geriatric and endocrinology/diabetes review after hospital discharge
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: conscious state and airway, hydration and blood pressure, glucose/osmolality and electrolyte correction, renal function, the urinary precipitant, and a full medication review with a documented sick-day plan
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any drop in consciousness, airway compromise, vomiting, or failure of the ambulance to arrive promptly — call 000 back immediately

must_include_items: Recognise a life-threatening hyperglycaemic emergency from 'HI' glucose plus altered mental status and profound dehydration
Escalate immediately by calling 000 for an emergency ambulance for hospital IV fluid resuscitation
Direct carer-deliverable measures: lay him flat with legs elevated, protect/monitor the airway, recovery position if consciousness drops further
Advise withholding further oral diabetes tablets and NOT forcing oral fluids in a drowsy patient
Explicitly keep DKA, stroke and sepsis in view and treat the likely urinary precipitant as part of hospital care
acceptable_alternatives: Framing the emergency as 'very high blood sugar with dehydration and confusion needing an ambulance' without naming HHS is acceptable, provided escalation and carer measures are correct.
errors_of_omission: Failing to escalate to 000 / emergency transfer
Failing to give airway-protection and positioning advice for a drowsy patient
Not recognising the severity because the onset was gradual
errors_of_commission: Managing as DKA with aggressive insulin, or advising insulin before fluids
Instructing staff to force oral fluids into an obtunded patient (aspiration risk)
Continuing metformin/gliclazide through the acute illness
Routing to a stroke pathway or treating a UTI alone while missing the hyperglycaemic emergency
Under-triaging to a GP/urgent-care review instead of an ambulance
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T5 emergency case: a pass REQUIRES recognition of the hyperglycaemic emergency AND immediate 000 escalation. Omitting escalation, or advising insulin/forced oral fluids at home, is an automatic FAIL regardless of other content.
